_metadata.scope: Canonical 101 grapes from Body of Wine / Grape Affinities (locked 2026-05-10)

reds.Aglianico: The Greek inheritance of southern Italy; a grape of black tannin and volcanic structure, slow to open and slow to forget, planted on Vesuvius's and Vulture's old fires.
reds.Baga: Bairrada's difficult red; thin-skinned and thick-tannined at once, long mistaken for a rustic also-ran until the producers who took its patience seriously revealed something else.
reds.Barbera: Piedmont's everyday red; the wine for the weekday table beside Nebbiolo's solemn weekend, all acid and red-fruit lift, structured enough for ambition when ambition arrives.
reds.Blaufränkisch: Burgenland's signature; a dark, peppery, structured red whose three borders and three names map a single Central European belt, the cool-climate equivalent to Syrah's warmer extroversion.
reds.Bobal: Spain's interior endurance; a grape that the meseta planted for volume and the meseta's altitude turned, in old vines, into something with bone and depth.
reds.Cabernet Franc: The cooler Cabernet; the grape Bordeaux blends and the Loire takes alone, herbal and graphite-leaned where its more famous offspring goes opulent and dark.
reds.Cabernet Sauvignon: The world's serious red; thick-skinned and slow to ripen, demanding a long warm autumn and rewarding it with the structure that has anchored Bordeaux and Napa and so many imitators.
reds.Carignan: The Mediterranean's survivor; planted in volume across Languedoc and Priorat, dismissed for decades, drunk again now because the old bush vines never stopped saying what they always had to say.
reds.Carménère: The lost Bordeaux; thought extinct in France until 1994, when Chile recognised it had been growing the variety under another name for over a century — green-edged, savoury, distinctly itself.
reds.Cinsault: The Mediterranean's light red; pale and perfumed, low in tannin and high in charm, the grape that the warm south makes for everyday pleasure and the careful winemaker makes for surprise.
reds.Corvina: Valpolicella's pale heart; thin-skinned and bright on its own, transformed by the appassimento drying-loft into the dense, raisined depth of Amarone.
reds.Counoise: Châteauneuf-du-Pape's gentle minority; a peppery, lifted grape that softens Grenache's weight in the blend and brings perfume where the warm south might otherwise insist on power.
reds.Dolcetto: Piedmont's first wine of the year; ripens before Nebbiolo, drinks before Nebbiolo, and asks no comparison — soft, dark, and complete on its own terms within months of harvest.
reds.Frappato: Sicily's light; a fragrant, low-tannin red that drinks closer to a pale rosé than to its island neighbours, partnered with Nero d'Avola in Cerasuolo di Vittoria.
reds.Gamay: The grape Burgundy banished in 1395 and Beaujolais took in; granite-rooted, juicy when made simply and serious when given the cru hillsides it always deserved.
reds.Garnacha Tintorera: The teinturier — red flesh as well as red skin; a 19th-century cross that gave bulk blends colour and now, in Spanish old-vine plantings, makes wines of its own dark gravity.
reds.Graciano: Rioja's perfumed minority; a small-yielding, acid-bright grape blended in for lift, nearly abandoned a generation ago and now bottled alone by producers who saw what was being lost.
reds.Grenache: The Mediterranean's generous red; thin-skinned and late-ripening, beloved across Châteauneuf, Priorat, and the old Barossa bush vines as a kind of common ground for the warm-climate sublime.
reds.Kadarka: Hungary's recovered red; the historic backbone of Eger's Bull's Blood, abandoned for easier varieties and now drawn back by producers who recognise what the modern table is ready for.
reds.Lagrein: Alto Adige's dark indigenous red; a relative of Syrah by genetic accident, deeply coloured and broad-shouldered, holding alpine freshness inside a warmer southern frame.
reds.Limniona: Thessaly's recovered ancient; an indigenous Greek red rescued from near-extinction in the early 2000s, structured and aromatic enough that comparisons reach for Syrah and Blaufränkisch.
reds.Malbec: The grape that left Cahors and found Mendoza; transformed by altitude and ultraviolet light into a plusher, plummier wine than the dark, tannic French original it descends from.
reds.Mavrud: Bulgaria's noble; the Thracian Valley's signature red, dark and tannic, with a structural seriousness that has carried the country's wine identity across centuries of upheaval.
reds.Mencía: Northwestern Spain's perfumed answer; old slate vineyards in Bierzo and Ribeira Sacra producing aromatic, mineral-lit reds long mistaken for Cabernet Franc until DNA proved its Iberian independence.
reds.Merlot: The right-bank softener; warmer, plummier, and earlier-ripening than its Cabernet counterpart, achieving its peak on the clay-limestone of Pomerol and Saint-Émilion and made ubiquitous everywhere else.
reds.Monastrell: The Spanish name for Mourvèdre, in its warmest home; ungrafted bush vines on sandy, phylloxera-free soils in Jumilla and Yecla producing concentrated wines of meat, leather, and southern heat.
reds.Montepulciano: Central Italy's everyday backbone; richly fruited and softly tannined Abruzzo reds at one end, structured Colline Teramane bottlings at the other, the grape generous enough to do both.
reds.Mourvèdre: The late bloomer of Mediterranean reds; needs extreme heat, austere in youth, and capable after a decade of the gamiest, most haunted complexity the Rhône and Bandol allow.
reds.Nebbiolo: The pale grape that holds Piedmont's most serious arguments; tar, rose, cherry, and a tannic structure that demands years before it consents — named for the Langhe's autumn fog.
reds.Negroamaro: Puglia's black-bitter, the literal translation of its name; a southern Italian variety carrying fig, tobacco, and Mediterranean heat in old bush-vine plantings on the Salento peninsula.
reds.Nerello Mascalese: Etna's volcanic red; pale and tannic, high-acid, called the Pinot Noir of the south, expressing the volcano's contrade with a precision that has reshaped southern Italian winemaking.
reds.Nero d'Avola: Sicily's flagship; the warm, dark, plummy red that defined the island's modern identity, ranging from everyday accessibility to structured cuvées positioning Sicily as a serious estate-wine region.
reds.Petit Verdot: Bordeaux's darkest blending partner; tiny berries, late ripening, contributing intense colour and graphite-violet aromatics — small percentages on the Left Bank, increasingly varietal in warmer New World homes.
reds.Petite Sirah: California's old workhorse; a 19th-century French cross of Syrah and Peloursin that found its lasting home in Lodi and Paso Robles, producing inky wines of blueberry and dark spice.
reds.Pinot Noir: The grape that disappears into its terroir; loved equally by Burgundy's devotees, Willamette's idealists, and Santa Cruz's obsessives, in three different keys.
reds.Pinotage: South Africa's invention; a 1925 Stellenbosch cross of Pinot Noir and Cinsault, splitting drinkers between admirers of its singular character and detractors put off by smoke-and-coffee intensity.
reds.Plavac Mali: Dalmatia's signature red; a natural offspring of Zinfandel's ancestor and Dobričić, grown on the sun-scorched Pelješac terraces above the Adriatic, producing structured wines of fig, herb, and Mediterranean heat.
reds.Poulsard: The Jura's translucent red; so pale it sometimes reads as a dark rosé, vinified under a yeast veil into nutty oxidative wines, prized by sommeliers for its regional eccentricity.
reds.Primitivo: The Puglian half of the Zinfandel story; the same Croatian-descended grape, grown in the warmer flat vineyards of southern Italy, producing jammy, high-alcohol reds with dried fig and chocolate.
reds.Sagrantino: Umbria's wall of tannin; one of the most phenolically concentrated grapes anywhere, native to the hills around Montefalco, requiring extended ageing before its formidable structure becomes drinkable.
reds.Sangiovese: The soul of Tuscany; sour-cherry-bright, herb-edged, exquisitely site-sensitive, producing wines from everyday Chianti to the slow-aging gravity of Brunello di Montalcino.
reds.Schiava: Alto Adige's lightest red; pale, low-tannin, almost transparent, the alpine variety of strawberry and almond once dominant in the region and now persisting as the local everyday glass.
reds.St. Laurent: The Pfalz's quiet red; an Austrian-German grape grown for centuries at communal tables before anyone thought to call it serious.
reds.Syrah: Northern Rhône's signature; pepper, black fruit, smoked meat in the cool climate of Côte-Rôtie and Hermitage, becoming Shiraz when Australia warms it into something richer and broader-shouldered.
reds.Tannat: Madiran's iron grape; one of the most tannic varieties ever drunk, so structured that micro-oxygenation was invented for it, finding its softer second home in Uruguay's Atlantic-tempered vineyards.
reds.Tempranillo: Spain's most adaptable noble; backbone of Rioja and Ribera del Duero, taking on oak's vanilla in traditional houses and stripped purity in the modernists, known by half a dozen names.
reds.Tinta Roriz: The Portuguese name for Tempranillo, working the schist terraces of the Douro; a structural presence in Port and dry red blends, well-suited to the valley's extreme heat and cold.
reds.Touriga Franca: The Douro's reliable contributor; floral, red-fruited, drought-resistant, planted more widely than any other Port grape, providing perfume and elegance where Touriga Nacional brings concentration.
reds.Touriga Nacional: Portugal's crown jewel; small yields, deep concentration, intoxicating aromatics of violet and wild rose, the grape that anchors the country's best Port and increasingly its great dry Douro reds.
reds.Trousseau: The Jura's fuller-bodied red beside the paler Poulsard; thin-skinned, low-yielding, planted on the region's warmest south-facing limestone, revived by the natural wine movement as Jurassien terroir's vehicle.
reds.Xinomavro: Greece's Nebbiolo-haunted noble; pale and tannic and slow, named for its acidity ("sour-black"), capable of world-class longevity in Naoussa and a more delicate Pinot-like register in higher-altitude Amyntaio.
reds.Zinfandel: California's heritage red; arrived from Croatia in the 1850s as Crljenak Kaštelanski, perfected in old bush-vine plantings of Lodi and Dry Creek, brambly at one end, jammy at the other.
reds.Zweigelt: Austria's everyday red; a 1922 crossing of Blaufränkisch and St. Laurent, cherry-bright and softly tannined, the country's most-planted variety and a reliable producer's anchor across Burgenland.

whites.Albariño: Galicia's Atlantic white; thick-skinned and aromatic, grown on granite under the rain-laden western Iberian sky, holding salt and stone fruit in equal measure.
whites.Aligoté: Burgundy's patient white; the grape kept growing on the slopes that didn't make the cut for Pinot, ripening late into wines of unexpected nerve.
whites.Arinto: Portugal's acid-keeper; a grape that holds its sharpness in heat where most whites soften, planted from cool Bucelas to the burning Alentejo as the backbone of Portuguese white blends.
whites.Arneis: Roero's rascal; rescued from near-extinction in the 1980s, an aromatic and textural white that became Piedmont's other serious answer alongside Cortese.
whites.Assyrtiko: Santorini's volcanic spine; ungrafted bush vines coiled against the wind on pumice and ash, producing whites of citrus and saline minerality that hold their acid through the Mediterranean heat.
whites.Chardonnay: The grape that goes everywhere and adapts to everything; from Chablis's steel to Meursault's richness to Sonoma's sun to Tasmania's chill, the most versatile white the world grows.
whites.Chasselas: Switzerland's mirror; a famously neutral grape that the Swiss argue expresses terroir more clearly than any aromatic variety, producing wines of stone, flower, and alpine clarity.
whites.Chenin Blanc: The Loire's shape-shifter; capable of bone-dry, off-dry, sweet, and sparkling from the same vineyards, its acidity preserving everything, its aging arc among the longest in white wine.
whites.Clairette: Provence's old soft white; one of the ancient Rhône varieties, contributing flower and roundness to white blends across the warm Mediterranean, vulnerable to oxidation but valued where carefully handled.
whites.Colombard: Gascony's distillation grape, in second life; bred for the Cognac and Armagnac stills for its acid, now also bottled as a crisp, citrus-bright wine in South Africa and southwest France.
whites.Cortese: Piedmont's chalk-soil white; the grape of Gavi, lean and citrus-bright with almond on the finish, the structural counterpart to Roero's softer, more aromatic Arneis.
whites.Encruzado: The Dão's serious white; an oak-friendly Portuguese variety that combines citrus freshness with the textural mid-palate and granite-mineral lift that mark the region's best whites.
whites.Falanghina: Campania's old white, possibly descended from the Falernian wines of imperial Rome; aromatic, citrus-and-tropical-fruit forward, bright on volcanic Campi Flegrei soils close to Naples.
whites.Fiano: Irpinia's age-worthy white; complex, textural, with hazelnut and honey developing slowly in bottle, the southern Italian variety that most clearly stands beside the great whites of the north.
whites.Friulano: Friuli's signature white; almond-edged and herbal, grown on the flysch hills the Italian-Slovenian border crosses without dividing, anchoring one of Europe's most cross-cultural wine regions.
whites.Furmint: Tokaj's other voice; the grape that holds sweetness and severity in the same bottle.
whites.Garganega: Soave's pale heart; light and almond-scented from the volcanic Classico hills, much-abused by mass-market plains production but capable, in serious hands, of mineral depth that rivals fine Burgundy.
whites.Gewürztraminer: Alsace's perfumed extrovert; lychee and rose petal and Turkish delight, low in acid, polarising in personality, the variety that knows what it is and asks the drinker to decide.
whites.Godello: Galicia's rescued white; reduced to fewer than a hundred hectares in the 1970s, now the most exciting white of northwestern Spain, combining Chardonnay's body with Albariño's freshness on slate soils.
whites.Greco: Campania's other Hellenic inheritance; grown on tuff-rich volcanic hills in Irpinia, producing structured, mineral whites that age decade-long into honey and lanolin alongside Fiano as Magna Graecia's surviving voices.
whites.Grüner Veltliner: Austria's signature white; peppery and citrus-bright in everyday form, structured and age-worthy from the Wachau's primary-rock terraces, the country's most planted variety and its most serious.
whites.Loureiro: Northern Portugal's perfume; an aromatic white of orange blossom, acacia, and citrus from the green, rain-drenched Minho, capturing one of Iberia's coolest and wettest landscapes in a single bottle.
whites.Malagousia: Greece's rescued aromatic; saved by a single producer in the 1970s, full-bodied and floral with jasmine and ripe peach, the variety that proved indigenous Greek whites could be world-class.
whites.Malvasia: Not one grape but a Mediterranean family named for a Greek port, the wines ranging from dry Istrian whites to the sweet wines of the Aeolian islands.
whites.Marsanne: The Northern Rhône's full-bodied white; rich, low-acid, almond and beeswax-textured, paired traditionally with Roussanne in Hermitage and Saint-Joseph, the slower-aging white that asks the drinker to wait.
whites.Mauzac: Gaillac's old white; the variety of Blanquette de Limoux, possibly the oldest documented sparkling wine, light and apple-bright with a rustic edge the region has never tried to soften.
whites.Melon de Bourgogne: Muscadet's only grape; expelled from Burgundy and rooted in the granitic soils near Nantes, mineral and saline, traditionally aged on its lees for the creamy texture that grounds Atlantic seafood.
whites.Muscat Blanc: The grape that smells like grapes; the noblest of the ancient Muscat family, producing dry Alsatian Muscat at one end and sweet Beaumes-de-Venise at the other, possibly the oldest cultivated variety.
whites.Müller-Thurgau: Germany's mid-century compromise; a Riesling crossing engineered for reliability and once the country's most planted variety, now stepping back into a quieter role as Riesling reclaims the serious sites.
whites.Petit Manseng: Jurançon's concentrated white; small thick-skinned berries that shrivel on the vine into intensely flavoured wines, dry or sweet, of exotic fruit and acidity that hold a decade of cellaring.
whites.Pinot Blanc: Pinot Noir's white mutation; medium-bodied and gently aromatic, the everyday alternative in Alsace and one of Alto Adige's most successful whites, capable of unexpected richness in old vines.
whites.Pinot Gris: The same grape Italy and Alsace drink differently; pale, neutral Pinot Grigio from the northeast Italian plains at one end, rich and copper-tinted Alsatian Tokay at the other.
whites.Ribolla Gialla: Friuli's amber-wine grape; the variety the orange wine movement reclaimed through extended skin-contact maceration, producing tannic, dried-fruit-and-tea-flavored wines on the Italian-Slovenian borderlands.
whites.Riesling: The grape most transparent to where it grows; from the Mosel's slate to Alsace's limestone to the Wachau's loess, the white that wine writers most often call the greatest.
whites.Roussanne: The Northern Rhône's higher-acid white; the aromatic, structural partner to Marsanne, harder to grow and more rewarding, named for the russet colour of its ripe berries.
whites.Sauvignon Blanc: The grape that smells before it tastes; cut grass, gooseberry, passionfruit — the variety of Sancerre's chalk and Marlborough's coastal sun, recognisable across a room before the glass reaches the lip.
whites.Silvaner: Franken's understated white; an older German variety overshadowed by Riesling, producing dry, earthy, mineral wines on chalky soils, the perfect match for the heartier food its culture grew up around.
whites.Sémillon: The grape that transforms; supremely susceptible to noble rot in Sauternes, blended with Sauvignon Blanc for great dry Bordeaux whites, capable in Hunter Valley of decade-long evolution into toast and honey.
whites.Torrontés: Argentina's signature white; a natural Muscat cross producing intensely perfumed wines of rose petal and orange blossom from the high-altitude vineyards of Salta, floral opulence held in check by altitude-driven acidity.
whites.Trebbiano: The world's most prolific white; planted across central Italy and southwestern France, generally lean and neutral, occasionally rising into something extraordinary in the hands of producers who take it seriously.
whites.Ugni Blanc: France's most planted white, almost entirely for distillation; high-acid and neutral, the variety the Cognac and Armagnac stills are built around, rarely seen as table wine.
whites.Verdejo: Rueda's signature; aromatic and herbaceous with fennel and bitter almond, rescued in the 1970s through cold fermentation from a region that had been making oxidised, fortified-style wines.
whites.Verdelho: Madeira's medium-dry voice; one of the four noble grapes of the island's fortified tradition, also producing aromatic dry whites in mainland Portugal and a tropical version in Australia.
whites.Verdicchio: The Marche's saline white; medium-bodied, citrus and almond and a touch of sea, capable in Riserva form of developing honey and lanolin complexity that rivals fine Chablis.
whites.Vermentino: The western Mediterranean's coastal white; saline and citrus-bright across Sardinia, Liguria, Corsica, and Provence, the variety that captures the feeling of seafood beside the sea in a single grape.
whites.Vidiano: Crete's emerging white; saved from obscurity by a few quality-minded producers, medium-bodied with stone fruit and honey, growing in critical attention as Greece rediscovers its indigenous heritage.
whites.Viognier: Condrieu's perfumed gift; opulent, low-acid, apricot and orange blossom, nearly extinct in the 1960s with fourteen hectares left, now planted globally but most itself in Northern Rhône granite.
whites.Welschriesling: Central Europe's reliable white, unrelated to Riesling; widely planted across Austria, Hungary, Croatia, and Slovenia, mostly fresh and everyday in dry form, occasionally rising to greatness in botrytised sweet wines from the Neusiedlersee.
